notes: R09 (negative slice) cited but input contains only .rstrip() method calls, no negative-index slicing. Minor rule-citation mismatch; all other dims clean.
structuralLevels typed as [][2]interface{} with runtime type assertions loses the tuple[str,int] static safety; a reviewer should confirm callers pass compatible values.

D3: _VERB_RE is referenced in input but never defined; a different model could choose any word list. D2: the output invents a specific verbRE word list that may not match the actual module-level _VERB_RE, introducing behavioral drift. Two dims at 2 -> Review.
Input is 9 lines with no outer loop or complex multi-rule transform; too simple for 14B tier. Single dim at 1 on D5 -> Review.
R11 (truthiness checks) cited but the Python body contains no Python-truthiness patterns; it is a pure range/len iteration with struct field copies. One spurious rule citation; all other dims clean.
Output uses '_ = strings.Join' as an import anchor to prevent unused-import compile error, but strings is not otherwise needed in the function. Minor fixable code smell in non-critical path.
Running() is hardcoded to return false with a TODO comment, silently dropping the Python .is_alive() behavior. The instruction explicitly asks to transform the 'running' property. D2=1 -> Reject.
Input is only 6 lines (def + 4 body lines + return). Transformation is a single goroutine launch; too simple for 14B tier. Single dim at 1 on D5 -> Review.

Input is a bare code fragment (no function signature); the return type of the surrounding function must be inferred from context. Instruction names the phase clearly but a different model might disagree on return signature. One D3=2, all others 3 -> Accept.

Name resolution uses fmt.Sprintf("%v", nameVal) which produces the string '<nil>' for a nil interface; the guard 'name == "<nil>"' is a fragile hack that may miss other zero-value representations. Minor behavioral drift in the fallback path.

D5=1: input is 9 lines (<10 line threshold); D6=2: core batching loop pattern near-identical to ID 26 (_embed_chunks) -- same range(0,len,batch_size) idiom, same sentence_transformers library, only minor rule differences (R08/R11 absent, R12 added)
D5=1: input is 8 lines (<10 line threshold); no statement-level branches in a sub-10-line snippet

D2=2: inner run() body is a stub placeholder (correctly scoped per instruction, but pipeline body not preserved); D4=2: R01 cited as '(free function)' but R01 maps class→struct; input is already a free function, no class instance visible

D2=2: Python _run() creates its own Store internally (store = Store(db_path_, read_only=False)); Go run() takes store as a parameter, changing store lifetime ownership semantics -- caller now manages lifecycle that Python inner function managed itself

D4=2: R08 (f-string → fmt.Sprintf) cited with parenthetical 'no f-strings but phase string literals preserved' -- rule has no visible application instance in input; citing a rule to note its inapplicability is a rule-citation defect

D4=2: R06 (params may be nil → nil check) cited but Python passes params directly to SqlAlchemyTransport with no nil guard; no visible nil-check instance in input; D5=1: 21 lines but zero statement-level branches -- pure linear delegation pipeline

D6=2: same NameSQLPair struct, same for-range-over-pairs pattern, and same chunk/annotate/enrich pipeline as ID 47 (load_from_cassandra); both examples teach the same dict|list → []NameSQLPair + per-item pipeline transformation

D6=2: same NameSQLPair struct, same for-range-over-pairs pattern, and same chunk/annotate/enrich pipeline as ID 46 (load_from_db); Cassandra-specific params (CassandraOptions struct) add some novelty but the core transformation pattern is near-identical
15-line input but no branching or loops in the Python body — two sequential calls with field access. Transformation is non-trivial (error propagation added) so D5 scores 2 rather than 1.

Instruction claims 'optionally runs NER' but the Python input snippet shows no NER code; run_ner/spacy_model params are forwarded but never exercised in either input or output. D3:2 ambiguity about NER scope; D2:2 because NER behavior is documented but absent from both sides consistently.
R03 cited (try/except -> error return) but the 10-line Python input has no try/except block — it calls methods directly with no error handling visible. R03 has no visible instance in this input.

D3: instruction says 'represent pa_type as a string tag' but output uses an iota enum struct — inconsistency between instruction and output design. D4: R05 (set->map[string]struct{}) cited but the Python input has no set or list — it returns a plain string with no collections.

The sklearn AgglomerativeClustering call is replaced with a placeholder (all-zero labels). The instruction acknowledges this is a 'pure-Go skeleton', but the clustering core behavior is not preserved — D2:2 since a reviewer must confirm this stub approach is acceptable.
R05 (set->map[string]struct{}) cited but the Python input operates only on int/float arguments with no set or list visible. R05 has no visible instance in this input.
Only 6 input lines — a single map lookup and one-line list comprehension filter. No statement-level loops or branches in the body beyond the nil guard. Too simple for 14B tier.

Only 3 input lines — a one-liner returning a dict.get() result. No loops or branches at all. Too simple for 14B tier (M6). Also a near-identical getter pattern to ID 68 (both are simple single-lookup ClusterMap methods returning nil for missing key — M7).

Only 5 input lines — a two-level for loop with no conditionals. Below the 10-line threshold and no branching; too trivial for 14B tier (M6). D6:2 because the label-formatting pattern (cluster_%04d) is nearly identical to ID 71 in this batch — same fmt.Sprintf pattern on a cluster index loop.

The cohesion-score computation is a direct extraction of the inner block from ID 67 (buildFromAssignments), which already transforms the same nested pair loop and mean calculation. Near-duplicate transformation pattern with ID 67.

Input is 6 lines combining three distinct fragments (idx comprehension, dist matrix init, result map). D5:1 — below 10 lines with no branches. D6:1 — the buildEntityIndex and labelsToClusterMap helpers are direct extractions of code already fully present in ID 65 (agglomerative), and buildDistMatrix duplicates the matrix-init logic from the same source. M6 (too simple) and M7 (duplicate of ID 65 content).

D2:2 — Python opens its own DB connection and closes it in finally; Go receives an already-open *sql.DB, shifting resource lifecycle to the caller. D4:2 — R07 (with stmt -> defer close) is cited but the Go output does not defer db.Close() because the connection is caller-managed; the rule is cited but not applied.

Reject: IsBuilding() references atomic.LoadInt64(&b.buildCount) but the CommunityIndexBuilder struct only declares wg sync.WaitGroup and logger — buildCount int64 field is never defined, making the output non-compiling. R11 is cited for explicit nil/bool checks but the cited transformation (atomic counter for is_building) has no visible instance in the input; the Python only checks thread liveness. Input is also short (13 lines) with minimal branching.

Review: Input is the CommunityIndex __init__ plus the build() signature only (first 30 lines per instruction) — no logic or branching in the input, just field initialization and parameter declarations. The output defines struct and BuildParams but contains no executable function body. D5=1 because input has no statement-level loops or conditionals. D3 is slightly ambiguous (instruction says 'first 30 lines' of build, which is only the signature), but stays at 2 since the output can still be derived.

Review: Dead code bug in output — after the first `if err != nil { ... }` block which handles multilevel error, the `} else if err != nil {` at the end is unreachable since err was already confirmed non-nil and handled in the prior block. The multilevel error path silently falls through to populate flat attrs instead of returning an error, causing behavioral drift from Python's re-raise behavior.

Minor: Python uses named .format(topic_label=..., n_chunks=..., chunks=...) but Go uses positional fmt.Sprintf with %s placeholders. If the default template uses {topic_label}/{n_chunks}/{chunks} named keys, the fmt.Sprintf call will not substitute correctly. Single non-critical behavioral drift — a reviewer should confirm the template constants use positional %s instead of named keys.
Review: Input is only 10 lines with no loops or statement-level branches — just two regex substitution calls with a nested function. Too simple for 14B tier.
Review: Input is 12 lines with no loops and only a single simple equality-check method. Field assignments plus one boolean check — no non-trivial logic. Too simple for 14B tier.
